basis: Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Belgian rooftop PV technical potential at 15.2% of total electricity consumption; translated to a moderate household ceiling because residential demand is a smaller denominator but dense/urban housing limits full household self-supply.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Denmark at 18.4% of total electricity consumption; moderate ceiling reflecting good building stock but lower winter PV output and heating-season mismatch.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Germany at 20.2% of total electricity consumption and identify Germany as a large economic-potential market; translated to a high-moderate household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Greece at 32.0% of total electricity consumption and >30% economic potential; translated to a high household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Spain at 28.0% of total electricity consumption and a large cost-competitive potential; translated to a high-moderate household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate France at 28.5% of total electricity consumption and a large cost-competitive potential; translated to a high-moderate household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Ireland at 11.2% of total electricity consumption; translated to a lower household ceiling because of weaker solar resource and winter mismatch.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Italy at 31.0% of total electricity consumption and >30% economic potential; translated to a high household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Luxembourg at 10.9% technical and 6.2% economic potential of total electricity consumption; translated to a constrained household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate the Netherlands at 16.7% technical potential; translated to a moderate ceiling, with dense housing and grid congestion limiting the practical household share.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Austria at 20.8% of total electricity consumption; translated to a high-moderate household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Portugal at 52.3% of total electricity consumption and 51.8% economic potential; translated to a very high household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Finland at 6.1% of total electricity consumption; translated to a low-moderate household ceiling because winter demand and seasonal mismatch are strong.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Sweden at 5.7% of total electricity consumption; translated to a low-moderate household ceiling because winter demand and seasonal mismatch are strong.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate the UK at 14.4% technical but only 2.1% economic potential under 2017 assumptions; translated to a lower-moderate household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Czechia at 23.7% technical potential but low cost-competitive potential; translated to a moderate physical ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Estonia at 17.1% technical potential and identify low tariffs as an economic barrier; translated to a moderate physical ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Cyprus at 119.8% of total electricity consumption and 115.6% economic potential; translated to the highest household ceiling, still capped below full self-supply.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Latvia at 22.1% technical potential but very low economic potential under 2017 assumptions; translated to a moderate physical ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Lithuania at 30.0% technical potential but very low economic potential under 2017 assumptions; translated to a high-moderate physical ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Hungary at 48.0% technical potential but finance/tariff barriers; translated to a high physical ceiling with economics left to the cost module.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Malta at 37.0% of total electricity consumption and 37.0% economic potential; translated to a high household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Poland at 23.3% technical potential but low economic potential under 2017 assumptions; translated to a high-moderate physical ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Slovenia at 20.8% technical potential; translated to a high-moderate household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Slovakia at 36.3% technical potential but very low economic potential under 2017 assumptions; translated to a high physical ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Bulgaria at 59.8% technical potential and note strong solar resource but tariff/finance barriers; translated to a high physical ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Romania at 82.3% technical potential but low economic potential under 2017 assumptions; translated to a very high physical ceiling.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 residential-rooftop methodology for high-latitude Europe plus Nordic seasonal mismatch; no EU Bodis country value available for Norway.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Western Europe residential-rooftop archetype and Alpine/urban constraints; no EU Bodis country value available for Switzerland.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 high-latitude residential-rooftop framework; low solar resource and strong seasonal mismatch imply a low practical household ceiling.
Bodis et al. 2019 estimate Croatia at 50.8% technical potential but low economic potential under 2017 assumptions; translated to a high physical ceiling.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Turkey/Middle East cost-region mapping and strong solar resource, moderated for urban apartment shares and grid limits.
Inferred from neighbouring Balkan values in Bodis et al. 2019 and the Gernaat et al. 2020 Central/Eastern Europe residential-rooftop archetype.

NREL 2016 estimates U.S. rooftop technical potential at 1,432 TWh/year, 39% of national electricity sales, with small buildings providing about 65% of rooftop potential; translated to a 60% household ceiling.
Gernaat et al. 2020 identify Japan as a high-rise residential archetype with a low roof-to-floor coefficient; translated to a constrained household ceiling.
Gernaat et al. 2020 map Canada to the U.S. cost/roof archetype; value reduced relative to the U.S. for lower winter PV coincidence and higher seasonal mismatch.
APVI/UNSW/ISF estimate Australian rooftop potential at 179 GW and 245 TWh/year, exceeding NEM consumption; translated to a very high but not full household ceiling.
Inferred from the Gernaat et al. 2020 Oceania residential-rooftop archetype, reduced relative to Australia for weaker solar resource and higher seasonal mismatch.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Russia residential-rooftop archetype; colder climate, lower irradiance, and district/apartment housing imply a lower ceiling.
Rest of Annex I inferred as a weighted conservative blend of Canada, Norway, Switzerland, Iceland, and other high-income non-EU regions in Gernaat et al. 2020.

Chinese rooftop studies report very large urban rooftop potential, including estimates around 672 TWh/year for urban roofs; translated to a high-moderate household ceiling after allowing for high-rise housing and urban grid limits.

Gernaat et al. 2020 identify India as among the lowest-cost rooftop PV regions and global residential-rooftop potential is large relative to residential demand; translated to a very high household ceiling.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Mexico/Latin America rooftop-cost archetype, high solar resource, and comparatively low-rise urban form.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Brazil/Latin America archetype with strong solar resource and large residential roof potential.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Rest of South America archetype; good resource but more temperate load/resource profile than tropical Latin America.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Rest of South/Central America archetype and equatorial solar resource, moderated for urban apartment shares.
Rest of Latin America inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Mexico, Brazil, Rest Central America, and Rest South America archetypes.
Gernaat et al. 2020 maps Korea with Japan-like rooftop cost/urban form; high-rise housing constrains household rooftop self-supply.
Inferred from Japan/Korea high-density East Asian archetype in Gernaat et al. 2020, with strong solar resource offset by dense urban form.
Inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Indonesia/Southeast Asia archetype; strong solar resource but dense urban areas and grid constraints reduce the practical ceiling.
Rest of ASEAN inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Southeast Asia archetype and World Bank-style rooftop technical-potential studies for Vietnam.

Rest of OPEC ex Venezuela inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Middle East and North Africa archetypes: excellent resource but apartment shares, tariffs, and export limits constrain household rooftop use.
Rest of World inferred from Joshi et al. 2021 global rooftop potential and Gernaat et al. 2020 regional residential-rooftop archetypes; conservative because the aggregate mixes diverse countries.
Ukraine inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Ukraine/Russia-region mapping and recent rooftop-resilience literature; good summer resource but cold-season mismatch limits full household self-supply.
Saudi Arabia inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Middle East archetype and excellent solar resource, moderated for high cooling loads, roof suitability, and grid/export constraints.
Nigeria inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Western Africa archetype and high decentralized-PV relevance, moderated for urban density and affordability constraints.
South Africa inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Southern Africa archetype and strong solar resource, moderated for income and distribution-network constraints.
North Africa OPEC inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Northern Africa/Middle East archetypes with excellent solar resource but practical grid and building constraints.
Central Africa OPEC inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Central/Western Africa archetypes; good resource but lower urban rooftop data quality and grid constraints.
Malaysia inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Southeast Asia archetype; high solar resource but dense housing and humid/cloudy conditions moderate the ceiling.
Kazakhstan inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Central Asia/Russia-region mapping; good continental solar in some areas but winter mismatch and urban form limit household rooftop supply.
Rest North Africa inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Northern Africa archetype and strong solar resource, moderated for grid/export and affordability constraints.
Rest Central Africa inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 African regional archetypes; value is conservative because country-level rooftop datasets are sparse.
Rest West Africa inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Western Africa archetype and high decentralized-PV relevance, moderated for affordability and grid constraints.
Rest East Africa inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Eastern Africa archetype and high solar resource, moderated for heterogeneous building stock.
Rest South Africa inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Southern Africa archetype and strong solar resource, moderated for affordability and distribution limits.
Egypt inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Northern Africa/Middle East archetypes and excellent solar resource, moderated for dense urban housing and grid/export constraints.
Democratic Republic of Congo inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Central Africa archetype; conservative due to sparse rooftop data, lower electrification, and implementation constraints.
Kenya inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Eastern Africa archetype and strong decentralized-solar relevance, moderated for affordability and urban roof constraints.
UAE inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Middle East archetype and very high solar resource, moderated for high-rise urban form, cooling demand timing, and distribution limits.
Pakistan inferred from Gernaat et al. 2020 Rest of South Asia archetype and strong solar resource; translated to a high household ceiling with practical limits below full self-supply.
